---
title: "Session 1"
author: "Dhruvi Sheth 22294"
date: "5 Jan, 2026"
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_notebook: default
---

```{r}
############################################################
# Session 1: What Is Biostatistics?
# Purpose: Visualize variation, sampling, and uncertainty
############################################################

# Clear workspace
rm(list = ls())

############################################################
# 1. Data as Recorded Observations
############################################################

# Built-in biological dataset
data(iris)

# Look at the structure
str(iris)

# Explain verbally:
# - Each row is a flower (individual)
# - Each column is a variable (measurement)

############################################################
# 2. Variation Is Everywhere
############################################################

# Extract one biological measurement
sepal_length <- iris$Sepal.Length

# Summary statistics
summary(sepal_length)

# Visualize variation
hist(sepal_length,breaks = 20,
     col = "lightblue",
     main = "Variation in Sepal Length",
     xlab = "Sepal Length (cm)")

# Teaching point:
# No two flowers are identical.

############################################################
# 3. Different Groups, Different Distributions
############################################################

boxplot(Sepal.Length ~ Species,
        data = iris,
        col = c("lightgreen", "lightyellow", "lightpink"),
        main = "Same Measurement, Different Species",
        ylab = "Sepal Length (cm)")

# Teaching point:
# Biological context matters.

############################################################
# 4. Population vs Sample
############################################################

# Treat the full dataset as a "population"
population_mean <- mean(sepal_length)

# Take a small sample
set.seed(42)
sample_10 <- sample(sepal_length, size = 10)

sample_mean <- mean(sample_10)

population_mean
sample_mean

# Teaching point:
# Sample estimates differ from population values.

############################################################
# 5. Sampling Variability
############################################################

# Repeat sampling many times
set.seed(123)

sample_means <- replicate(
  1000,
  mean(sample(sepal_length, size = 10))
)

# Plot sampling distribution
hist(sample_means,
     breaks = 30,col = "lightgray",
     main = "Sampling Distribution of the Mean",
     xlab = "Sample Mean")

abline(v = population_mean, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Teaching point:
# Statistics quantifies this uncertainty.

############################################################
# 6. Sampling Bias (WWII Story in Code)
############################################################

# Imagine planes with damage locations
# We only observe returning planes

damage <- c(rep("Wing", 80), rep("Engine", 5), rep("Tail", 15))

barplot(table(damage),
        col = "orange",
        main = "Observed Bullet Damage on Returning Planes")

# Teaching point:
# What we observe is biased by survival.

############################################################
# 7. Why Big Data Doesn't Remove Bias
############################################################

# Increase sample size
sample_means_big <- replicate(
  1000,
  mean(sample(sepal_length, size = 100))
)

hist(sample_means_big,
     breaks = 30,
     col = "lightblue",
     main = "More Data, Still Uncertainty")

abline(v = population_mean, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Teaching point:
# Precision improves, bias remains.############################################################
# 8. Key Takeaway
############################################################

cat("
Statistics is not about exact answers.
It is about understanding uncertainty
in biological systems.
")
```
